findings.title: Pick entry price uses same-day close even when snapshot ran pre-market
findings.description: priceAt(history, ticker, asOf) walks the daily history forward until entry.date > targetDate and returns the last entry <= targetDate. For asOf='2026-05-13', that returns the close of 2026-05-13. But picks-history/2026-05-13.json has asOf='2026-05-13T04:52:03.762Z' — i.e. ~05:00 UTC, BEFORE the US market open at 13:30 UTC. So the audit measures entry at a price the screener could not have transacted at on the snapshot date.
findings.mechanism: Picks were chosen using whatever live snapshot.regularMarketPrice was available pre-open (likely prior-day close or a stale quote). Walk-forward then sets p0 = same-day official close, which incorporates the day's full move. Forward returns are computed from that look-ahead-contaminated p0.
findings.reproducer: Inspect picks-history/2026-05-13.json asOf timestamp (04:52 UTC, before US open). Then verify scripts/walk-forward-perf.js evaluateVintage passes asOf.slice(0,10)='2026-05-13' to priceAt, which returns history['SPY-equivalent'][2026-05-13].close — a price not realized until 21:00 UTC that day.
findings.impact: Systematically biases reported alpha. Direction depends on whether the screener's entry quote was below (likely upward-biased alpha if intraday rallied) or above (downward-biased) the official close. With ~3 of 6 vintages snapshotted before US open (05/10, 05/11, 05/13), at least half of all reported alpha figures sit on this contaminated p0.
findings.suggested_fix: Use t+1 open or t+1 close as the entry price (skip-a-day convention). Either: (a) compute entry as price at the next trading day strictly > asOf, or (b) require the snapshot timestamp to be post-close (e.g. asOf hour > 21 UTC) and reject vintages that violate it.
findings.compound_hint: Combine with F-BT-005 — both inflate live-vs-backtest divergence.

findings.title: SPY benchmark always returns null — alphaVsSpy column is structurally empty
findings.description: computeBenchmarkReturn(history, asOf, days, 'SPY') is called for every vintage × horizon. But prices/history.json contains no SPY key (grep across the 70k-line file matches only QQQ, not SPY). Every spyReturn and alphaVsSpy is therefore null, which silently propagates into medianAlphaVsSpy = null and regimeAlpha.medianAlphaVsSpy = null.
findings.mechanism: priceAt returns null when history['SPY'] is undefined. returnPct(null, null) returns null. The median() of an empty array returns null. The methodology-report.md and walk-forward.md tables fill the SPY column with '—' for every row. The Markdown justly claims SPY is 'most honest' but the column never has data.
findings.reproducer: grep -c '"SPY"' prices/history.json -> 0. Run scripts/walk-forward-perf.js, inspect outputs/walk-forward.json: every alphaVsSpy field is null, every regimeAlpha[X].medianAlphaVsSpy is null.
findings.impact: The 'external US-equity reference' that walk-forward.md claims is the 'most honest' is permanently empty. All decisions documented as alpha-vs-SPY in methodology-report.md are actually based on nothing. Worse, regimeAlpha (Tag 139 BULL/BEAR/SIDEWAYS breakdown) uses 'alphaVsSpy' as its source field — so the entire macro-regime split is permanently {} / vacuous.
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) ensure SPY is fetched into prices/history.json (add SPY to the universe in the fetcher), or (b) fall back to QQQ which IS present, or (c) hard-fail with a clear error when SPY is missing instead of silently producing null. The current 'graceful — workflow still produces output' comment on line 122-123 is the bug.
findings.compound_hint: Combine with F-BT-003 — both undermine the documented 'three benchmarks' claim.

findings.title: Survivor-bias 'fix' is inactive: no picks-history file contains evaluatedTickers
findings.description: Tag 138 introduced a survivor-bias fix: when picksFile.evaluatedTickers is present, the universe-median benchmark is restricted to tickers that were actually evaluated at vintage time. snapshot-picks.js writes this field. BUT all five existing picks-history vintages (2026-05-08 through 2026-05-13) lack the field — grep on 'evaluatedTickers' across picks-history/ returns zero matches.
findings.mechanism: computeUniverseMedianReturn falls back to Object.keys(history) (today's full universe) when evaluatedTickers is missing/empty. Today's universe is by construction the set of tickers that still trade today — delisted/merged tickers from May 8 are not in history.json, so their (negative) forward returns never enter the benchmark. The universe-median is therefore upward-biased and alphaVsUniverse is artificially deflated. survivorBiasCorrected:false is emitted, but the methodology-report.md narrative still describes it as 'Tag 138: survivor-bias corrected when evaluatedTickers available' without flagging that none of the existing vintages have it.
findings.reproducer: grep -r 'evaluatedTickers' picks-history/ -> no matches. Then read scripts/walk-forward-perf.js:86-88: `const tickerList = (evaluatedTickers && evaluatedTickers.length > 0) ? evaluatedTickers : Object.keys(history);` — fallback path is taken for every vintage.
findings.impact: Every alphaVsUniverse number in outputs/walk-forward.json today is computed against today's survivors. The frozen-vintage fallback (also computed) is a much smaller sample (only picks themselves). The 'survivorBiasCorrected:false' flag is buried in the JSON, not surfaced in the markdown — readers see 'survivor-bias corrected' in the report header even when no vintage actually was.
findings.suggested_fix: (a) Backfill evaluatedTickers into existing picks-history files by reading methods-history (which has the full stock list per vintage). (b) Make methodology-report.md explicitly flag per-vintage whether survivorBiasCorrected is true/false, instead of claiming it generically. (c) Refuse to compute alphaVsUniverse when evaluatedTickers is missing — better silence than a wrong number.
findings.compound_hint: Combine with F-BT-002 — the only two benchmarks that remain (frozen-vintage and universe) are both compromised: frozen-vintage has tiny n, universe is uncorrected.

findings.title: Bootstrap CI seed depends only on array lengths — non-independent CIs across methods
findings.description: _rng(seed) is a fixed-seed LCG. bootstrapAlphaCI calls it with `_rng(passArr.length * 1000 + failArr.length)`. The seed is therefore a function of pass/fail group sizes only, not of the data values. Two different methods with the same (passN, failN) will get the IDENTICAL bootstrap resample index sequence.
findings.mechanism: Across ~30+ methods × 2 horizons, many methods will collide on (passN, failN) — e.g. all methods with passN=120, failN=90 share one RNG trajectory. Their CIs are then perfectly correlated (not independent), so the apparent breadth of 'methods with CI > 0' overstates evidence. Also the seed value (e.g. 120090) is in the LCG's transient regime — it isn't shuffled before first use, so the first ~5 draws are highly non-uniform.
findings.reproducer: Inspect bootstrapAlphaCI(passArr, failArr): the resample-index sequence (rng() * passArr.length | 0) is determined entirely by passArr.length and failArr.length, not by passArr/failArr contents. Construct two synthetic methods with the same group sizes but different data — their CI bounds will be drawn from the same percentile of resamples (relative percentiles, not absolute bounds).
findings.impact: Inflates apparent independence of method-effectiveness signals. If 12 methods all show 'CI lower bound > 0' that look like 12 independent rejections of H0, but they share resample structure, the joint significance is overstated. Karl's decision to weight or retire methods (methodology-report.md significant/flagged tables) rests on this.
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) hash the array contents into the seed (e.g. seed = sum-of-values | 0), or (b) use a global crypto.randomBytes-seeded RNG and forfeit reproducibility, or (c) use a proper Mersenne Twister and warmup. The comment 'fixed seed because we want reproducible audits' confuses reproducibility-of-the-same-input with statistical-independence-across-methods.
findings.compound_hint: Touches F-BT-006 — bootstrap with n<10 already gated, but the seed flaw applies to all surviving CIs.

findings.title: Calendar-day horizons land on weekends/holidays — silently use stale Friday close
findings.description: addDaysIso adds calendar days. For asOf='2026-05-08' (Fri) + 7 days = 2026-05-15 (Fri) — fine. But for asOf='2026-05-09' (Sat snapshot, which exists) + 7 days = 2026-05-16 (Sat) — no trading. priceAt then walks forward to find the last entry <= 2026-05-16, which is Friday 5/15's close, and accepts it because _daysBetween(5/15, 5/16) = 1 < PRICE_MAX_STALE_DAYS=7. So the '7-day forward return' is actually a 6-day return.
findings.mechanism: Calendar-day arithmetic + 7-day staleness slop means the effective horizon is variable: a 7d horizon can be anywhere from 5 to 9 trading days. A 28d horizon can be off by 1-2 trading days. A 84d horizon can be off by 4-6 trading days. The pickReturns and benchmarkReturn use the same priceAt, so they have similar (but not identical) date drift — alpha is approximately preserved, but the headline 'medianReturn' values are noisy.
findings.reproducer: evaluateVintage(picksFile_2026_05_09, history, regimes) calls addDaysIso('2026-05-09', 7) = '2026-05-16' (Sat). priceAt then returns the 2026-05-15 (Fri) close. Effective horizon = 6 calendar days / ~4 trading days, labeled '7d'.
findings.impact: Low-to-medium. Alphas are robust to a 1-2 day drift (both legs drift similarly), but the per-vintage medianReturn columns in walk-forward.md are mislabeled. The 84d horizon (12w) is the most affected — accumulated drift across the long window means returns can be off by ~1-2%.
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) convert horizons to trading days (use a calendar of US/global trading days) and step entry-by-entry, or (b) explicitly document that horizons are calendar days and that returns are 'first trading day on/after target'. Option (a) is the principled fix.
findings.compound_hint: Combine with F-BT-001 — both introduce date-boundary noise.

findings.title: MIN_VINTAGES=4 / MIN_SAMPLES_PER_GROUP=10 gate evidence-gate but point-alpha is still emitted
findings.description: When evidenceGate != 'ok' (insufficient vintages or insufficient samples), bootstrapAlphaCI is skipped and ciLo95/ciHi95 are null. But the point estimate `alpha = passMed - failMed` is still computed and written to method-effectiveness.json. Downstream consumers that read `alpha` without checking `evidenceGate` get a misleading number computed on n<10 or vintages<4.
findings.mechanism: _evalGroup returns alpha unconditionally on line 163 — `alpha: (passMed != null && failMed != null) ? passMed - failMed : null`. methodology-report.js line 92-94 reads `out.methods[m]['28d']` and exposes `alpha` in the 'Neutral' bucket. A method with n=3 vintages and passN=5 shows a definite-looking alpha number with no CI — readers eyeball it as 'small alpha' rather than 'insufficient'.
findings.reproducer: Run method-effectiveness on the 5-vintage snapshot. MIN_VINTAGES=4 means roughly half the methods that pass the vintage gate still fail the sample gate (passN or failN < 10). All produce non-null alpha with null CI. methodology-report bins them as 'neutral'.
findings.impact: Karl reads methodology-report.md and sees a sorted alpha list. Bottom-half methods have point alphas computed on <10 samples — these numbers are noise. The gate fires but doesn't suppress the point estimate.
findings.suggested_fix: Set alpha=null when evidenceGate != 'ok', or in methodology-report.js skip rendering alpha for non-'ok' rows. The simplest patch: `alpha: (insufficientVintages || insufficientSamples) ? null : ...` in _evalGroup.
findings.compound_hint: Combines with F-BT-004 — the methods that DO have CI may still have correlated CIs.

findings.title: pick-diff rolling Jaccard is labeled '4w' but actually rolls 5 daily snapshots
findings.description: Comment claims 'Rolling 4-week Jaccard average', but code does `picksVintages.slice(-5)` — the last 5 vintages. Snapshots happen daily (2026-05-08, 09, 10, 11, 13 are consecutive days), so this is a rolling 5-day window, not 4-week. The pairwise array has 4 entries between 5 vintages.
findings.mechanism: If snapshot cadence ever changes (e.g. moves to weekly), this silently becomes a 5-week window with no warning. The label in the JSON output (windowVintages=tickerSets.length) doesn't tell consumers the time span — only the count.
findings.reproducer: ls picks-history/ shows 5 dated files, all consecutive days. Read pick-diff.js:125 — slice(-5) takes those 5. Compute pairwise Jaccard between adjacent days. Output: rollingJaccard.HYPERGROWTH.meanJaccard = mean of 4 day-over-day comparisons, NOT week-over-week.
findings.impact: Whip-saw interpretation. Day-over-day Jaccard is typically very high (>0.9) — Karl will see 'rolling 4w mean = 0.92' and think the screener is highly stable across months, when it's actually only proving it's stable across 5 days.
findings.suggested_fix: Use a date-based window: filter vintages where asOf >= today - 28d, then compute pairwise. If cadence is daily, that's ~20 vintages; if weekly, ~4. Or rename to `rolling5VintageJaccard` to match the actual computation.

findings.title: methods-history quality field absent — entire byQuality split is empty noise
findings.description: The Phase-3.5 byQuality split reads `stockData.quality` from methods-history records. Inspection of methods-history/2026-05-13.json (the latest) shows no top-level `quality` field on stock entries — only nested fields like `quality-compounder-roic`, `hypergrowth-quality-class`, `margin-quality`. The byQuality.A/B/C/D buckets therefore receive zero data.
findings.mechanism: Line 131: `const quality = stockData.quality;` — this reads undefined for all stocks. Line 137: `if (quality && bucket.byQuality[quality])` — guard fails, nothing accumulates. The methodology-report.md byQuality table is unconditionally empty, but the section header 'Method-Effectiveness by Data-Quality Grade' still renders, suggesting the analysis ran.
findings.reproducer: Read methods-history/2026-05-13.json — no `quality` key directly under any stocks[ticker] entry. Inspect outputs/method-effectiveness.json after running — every byQuality is {} (rendered by line 186-187 only when entries exist, so the section is empty rather than misleading).
findings.impact: Either the methods-history writer was supposed to record `quality: 'A'|'B'|'C'|'D'` and doesn't, or this auditor was written against a future schema. The methodology-report.md template renders the section header anyway (line 140), so readers think the audit ran but found 'no methods have data across multiple quality grades yet'.
findings.suggested_fix: Either: (a) verify the methods-history writer emits stockData.quality, and if not, add it (likely sourced from a 'hypergrowth-quality-class' or similar method result); or (b) remove the byQuality logic until the upstream feature is built.

findings.title: Older picks-history vintages have missing score field — JSON drops null becomes undefined
findings.description: Picks in 2026-05-08.json have NO `score` field at all (e.g. lines 7-19 of that file end at `mustTotal` with no score). Picks in 2026-05-13.json have `score: 100` etc. The code at line 68 writes `score: (ev.score != null) ? Math.round(ev.score * 10) / 10 : null` — so even if ev.score were absent, it should write `score: null`. The field is entirely missing, suggesting strategy-modes evaluateMode returned an object without a `score` property at the time the older vintages were created.
findings.mechanism: JSON.stringify omits undefined-valued keys. If ev.score is undefined (vs null), the ternary returns null and the key is present. So the absence of the key means the object literal itself didn't set it — i.e. snapshot-picks.js code at the time of those vintages didn't have line 68. This is a schema discontinuity between vintages.
findings.reproducer: head picks-history/2026-05-08.json — first pick has no 'score' key. head picks-history/2026-05-13.json — first pick has 'score: 100'.
findings.impact: Walk-forward doesn't read score, so alpha math is unaffected. But pick-diff's whyDropped logic and any score-dependent sort assume the field exists. Schema-version unawareness means future audits that try to compute 'score trajectory' will silently treat missing-as-zero or skip the vintage.
findings.suggested_fix: Backfill old vintages by re-running the snapshot off cached snapshots (if available), OR add a schemaVersion field to picks-history files and have all consumers branch on it.

findings.title: getRegimeAt only looks backward 7 days — gaps silently degrade regime tagging
findings.description: If macro-regime.json has a gap > 7 days (workflow failure, holiday cluster), getRegimeAt returns null and the vintage's macroRegime is null. The vintage is then EXCLUDED from regimeAlpha aggregation (line 261: `vals = collect('alphaVsSpy', v => v.macroRegime === regime)` only sums matching regime). No warning is logged.
findings.mechanism: Walk-forward proceeds as if the vintage doesn't exist for regime analysis. The 'BULL/BEAR/SIDEWAYS' breakdown silently drops vintages. Combined with F-BT-002 (SPY always null), the regime split is doubly empty — but the markdown still renders an empty 'no 28d regime data' string.
findings.reproducer: Delete or skip a few dates from outputs/macro-regime.json. Run walk-forward-perf. Inspect outputs/walk-forward.json — affected vintages have macroRegime: null. They contribute to non-regime aggregates but not to regimeAlpha.
findings.impact: Low because F-BT-002 already neutralizes regimeAlpha. Once SPY is fixed, this becomes medium.
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) widen the lookback to 30 days with a warning, or (b) log when a vintage gets null regime, or (c) backfill the regime file before running.
findings.compound_hint: Combines with F-BT-002 — both undermine the Tag 139 regime feature.

findings.title: Backtest scoring uses today's Runner.evaluateStock — score logic drift is invisible to historical audit
findings.description: snapshot-picks.js calls Runner.evaluateStock(stock) and SM.evaluateMode at the time of the snapshot — so picks-history is correct for that moment. BUT method-effectiveness.js reads methods-history (which was also snapshotted at the time) and consumes the `pass` flag stored there. If a method's pass-threshold is later changed in code (e.g. fcf-yield threshold from 5% to 8%), historical methods-history is unchanged, but live picks now use the new threshold. The audit then reports alpha for the OLD definition of the method while production uses the NEW definition.
findings.mechanism: There's no version stamp on methods. method-effectiveness aggregates pass/fail across vintages assuming the method definition is stable. When methods are tuned (per CHANGELOG / Tag history this clearly happens), the alpha aggregation conflates two different methods under one methodId. The audit cannot detect when this happens — it just averages across the discontinuity.
findings.reproducer: Compare 2026-05-08 methods-history fcf-yield pass-rate vs 2026-05-13 fcf-yield pass-rate. If the threshold changed in between (check methods/fcf-yield.js git log), the pre/post records are aggregated as 'one method'.
findings.impact: Method-effectiveness alpha for any tuned method is a weighted average of multiple definitions. A method that was tightened and now has higher alpha will show muted total alpha because old-loose-version records drag the mean down.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `methodVersion` field to methods-history records (e.g. content-hash of the method file at evaluation time). Have method-effectiveness either (a) aggregate within a version only, or (b) report a version-change marker. Failing that, document a per-method 'as-of-version-X' window in the audit output.
findings.compound_hint: Combines with F-BT-006 — version drift + insufficient samples both inflate alpha estimate noise.

findings.title: walk-forward truncates picks to top-100 in snapshot — but evaluateVintage divides coverage by truncated count
findings.description: snapshot-picks.js stores only top-100 picks per mode (line 178: `const top100 = deduped.slice(0, 100)`). walk-forward then computes coverage = pickReturns.length / picks.length where picks.length is at most 100. If the actual pre-truncation pick count was 250, the audit cannot tell — `n: picks.length` reports 100, and a reader concludes 'mode had 100 picks' when actually it had 250 that got filtered.
findings.mechanism: Information loss at snapshot time. The truncation rank-orders by score and keeps the highest, so the audit measures alpha on the TOP picks only — which is what the screener delivers. That's actually defensible. The bug is the missing context: methodology-report.md says 'totalPicks' without disclosing the 100-cap.
findings.reproducer: Read snapshot-picks.js:178: top100 = deduped.slice(0,100). Read walk-forward.js:200: `out.modes[mode] = { n: picks.length, ... }`. The original deduped.length is lost.
findings.impact: Low. The alpha computed is on top-100, which IS what the screener serves. But the 'total picks' column suggests the screener had exactly that many candidates — readers may interpret 100 as an artifact of selectivity rather than truncation.
findings.suggested_fix: snapshot-picks.js should store `prunedFromN: deduped.length, retainedN: top100.length` as metadata. walk-forward should surface it.
